methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $200M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $1.8B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Watershed
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $1.8B

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Graphyte is a Series A climate and energy technology company founded by Barclay Rogers that has raised $57M to date against a current post-money valuation of $200M. The peer universe consists of 9 private companies in the same sector and stage, alongside 1 public comparable (LNZA). The three primary same-stage peers range from $110M to $1.8B, with Watershed, Fourth Power, and Steady Energy representing the core benchmarks.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using a 2.0x multiplier typical for Series A advancement, implies a valuation of $400M based on the $200M current post-money. Peer-median triangulation across the three same-sector, same-stage comparables yields $150M, with the broader peer range spanning $110M to $1.8B. The wide peer range reflects heterogeneity in capital intensity and business stage maturity within climate-tech.

The two methods produce a triangulated range of $128M (low) to $460M (high), with a median of $275M. The wide spread between peer-median ($150M) and stage-progression ($400M) reflects uncertainty about whether Graphyte's current valuation reflects or exceeds typical Series A pricing in a volatile sector. Key uncertainties include timing to next fundraise, the valuation treatment of comparable Watershed (which trades at a significant premium), and whether climate-tech multiples are normalizing. Further diligence on unit economics and capital efficiency relative to peers would help narrow the range.
